docs.title: Integration Docs
docs.subtitle: Learn how to integrate OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect into your application
docs.overviewP1: Sakura Account provides authentication and authorization services based on OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) standards. You can integrate it into any web app, mobile app, or backend service for unified user authentication.
docs.overviewP2: OAuth 2.0 is an open authorization protocol that allows third-party applications to access user resources with the user's consent, without exposing their password. OIDC adds an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0, providing standardized user identity information.
docs.endpointsDesc: The following are all OAuth/OIDC endpoints provided by Sakura Account:
docs.endpointAuthorizeDesc: Redirects users to authorize and obtain an authorization code
docs.endpointTokenDesc: Exchange an authorization code or refresh token for access tokens
docs.endpointUserinfoDesc: Retrieve user information using an access token
docs.endpointOpenIdConfigDesc: OIDC discovery endpoint, returns all endpoints and capabilities
docs.endpointJwksDesc: Returns the public key set for verifying JWT signatures
docs.flowDesc: The most commonly used OAuth 2.0 flow, suitable for web applications with a backend. The process has four steps:
docs.flowStep1Desc: Redirect the user to the authorization endpoint with your client information and requested scopes. The user will log in and confirm authorization at Sakura Account.
docs.flowStep2Desc: After the user approves, Sakura Account redirects back to your redirect_uri with an authorization code in the URL parameters.
docs.flowStep3Desc: Your backend exchanges the authorization code for an access token and refresh token at the token endpoint. This step happens server-side and is not exposed to the frontend.
docs.flowStep4Desc: Use the access token to call the UserInfo endpoint and retrieve the logged-in user's basic information (different fields are returned based on requested scopes).
docs.flowStep5Desc: When the access token expires (default 1 hour), use the refresh token to request a new access token from the token endpoint, without requiring the user to re-authorize. If the refresh token also expires (default 30 days), the user must go through the authorization flow again.
docs.flowNote: Note: The authorization code is single-use, expires immediately after use, and has a 10-minute validity period.
docs.scopesDesc: When initiating an authorization request, you can specify the user information you need via the scope parameter. Separate multiple scopes with spaces.
docs.scopeOpenidDesc: Required. Enables OpenID Connect flow and returns the user's unique identifier (sub). This information is included in the ID Token.
docs.scopeProfileDesc: Access the user's basic profile including username, nickname, avatar, and role.
docs.scopeEmailDesc: Access the user's email address and email verification status.
docs.scopesDefault: If no scope is specified, openid profile is used by default.
docs.codeDesc: Below are complete examples of implementing the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow in different languages. Replace YOUR_CLIENT_ID, YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET, and other placeholders with your actual application credentials.
docs.tokensDesc: After completing the authorization flow, you will receive the following tokens:
docs.tokenAccessDesc: Used to access protected resources. It's a JWT (JSON Web Token) with a default expiry of 1 hour (3600 seconds). Pass it in the Authorization header as a Bearer token.
docs.tokenRefreshDesc: Used to obtain a new Access Token when the current one expires, without requiring the user to re-authorize. Default expiry is 30 days. Store securely and never expose it.
docs.tokenIdDesc: Specific to OpenID Connect, contains user identity claims. It's a JWT signed with RS256. You can retrieve the public key from the JWKS endpoint for verification.
docs.tokenTypeInfo: Token type: Bearer. When calling APIs, add Authorization: Bearer '<access_token>' to the HTTP request headers.
docs.securityDesc: When integrating OAuth 2.0, please follow these security guidelines:
docs.securitySecretDesc: The Client Secret must only be used server-side. Never expose it in frontend code, Git repositories, or logs. If you suspect it's compromised, reset the secret immediately in the application details page.
docs.securityHttpsDesc: All OAuth endpoint communications must use HTTPS to prevent tokens and authorization codes from being intercepted during transmission.
docs.securityStateDesc: Pass a randomly generated state parameter in the authorization request and verify its consistency in the callback to prevent CSRF attacks.
docs.securityScopeDesc: Only request the scopes your application actually needs. For example, if you only need user login, use openid alone.
docs.securityTokenDesc: Access Tokens and Refresh Tokens should be stored securely. For web apps, use HttpOnly Cookies or server-side sessions. Avoid storing in localStorage.
docs.securityExpiryDesc: Use the Refresh Token to get a new Access Token when it expires. If the Refresh Token also expires, redirect the user to re-authorize.
